Bar Window Cabinet

A sample personalized presentation experience for a private residence: turning a technical bar-window drawing into a premium visual approval journey.

View Case See Assets
Scenario

The Harlow Residence

A client commissions a custom bar cabinet with fabricated metal windows. The fabrication team has technical drawings; the client needs confidence, mood, material clarity, and approval language.

ProjectCustom bar window cabinet
Client NeedUnderstand the finished piece before fabrication
Visual DirectorLesly Yankauskas
RoleClient Visualization & 3D Product Experience
From drawing to desire

Not a render. A decision system.

The client-facing site reframes the process: source drawings stay respected, but the customer sees scale, finish, mood, function, and context before committing.

01Early mockup for emotional alignment
02Polished approval visuals with correct scope labels
03Technical refinement after visual direction is approved
04Reusable marketing assets after the project
Input2D elevation
OutputApproval visual

“A technical drawing explains construction. A visualization experience explains value.”

Strategic positioning
Client Journey

The embedded workflow

Lesly’s role is to create a visual layer that sits between rough concept, shop drawing, client approval, and final fabrication.

1

Visual discovery

Collect drawings, client mood, space photos, desired material direction, and approval risks.

2

Fast mockup

Build a quick visual direction so the client can react before the team over-invests in final details.

3

Personalized micro‑site

Present the piece with mood, material, context, notes, and approval checkpoints in one elegant client page.

4

Technical fidelity pass

Once direction is approved, refine using final 2D/CAD specs, hardware, proportions, and material decisions.

5

Marketing conversion

Turn approved visuals into website, Instagram, proposal, and “from drawing to finished piece” assets.

Mood Direction

Private club, crafted metal, warm glow

The visual mood should feel aligned with bespoke metalwork, dark wood, architectural craft, and the emotional atmosphere of a custom interior piece.

Blackened SteelFrame / hardware
Walnut / MahoganyCabinet body
Architectural CraftMetal + space
Approval AtmosphereWarm interior glow
Walnut
Steel
Brass
Stone
Glow
Sample Presentation Assets

What the client sees

Each visual has a job: align emotion, confirm structure, show context, or move the approval forward.

45° inspection view

Depth, presence, material richness, and side profile.

Frontal approval

Client checks symmetry, proportions, and closed window layout.

Context study

Optional placement or social preview outside a studio/showroom.

Technical source

Preserves frame logic, arched transom, and door proportions.

Movement logic

Top view used to understand closed/open door behavior.

Client notes

Specs, unknowns, and assumptions stay visible.

Approval Language

One page, three decision modes

Client View

“This is the mood, scale, finish direction, and presence of the finished bar cabinet in your home.”

  • Approve the overall visual direction.
  • React to blackened steel, dark wood, warm glass, and stone.
  • Confirm whether the piece feels premium enough for the space.

Studio View

“This visual explains the project clearly enough to reduce ambiguity before final detailing.”

  • Identify what still needs material or hardware confirmation.
  • Keep visual revisions separate from fabrication revisions.
  • Create reusable presentation assets for future clients.

Fabrication View

“This is not the replacement for shop drawings. It is the approval layer that supports them.”

  • Use final CAD/PDF dimensions before fabrication-accurate renders.
  • Label concept visuals separately from production visuals.
  • Keep exact drawings as the source of truth for build details.
Client Approval Checklist

What the client can approve today

  • Overall mood: refined private-bar atmosphere with handcrafted materiality.
  • General cabinet proportions, arched opening, and four closed metal-framed windows.
  • Material direction: dark stained wood, blackened steel, stone top, warm interior glow.
  • Presentation direction for the next technical fidelity pass.

Important: final fabrication dimensions, lattice decisions, hardware details, and finish samples still require verified technical approval.

Director-level positioning

Lesly’s role

Director of Client Visualization & 3D Product Experience

She owns the visual decision layer: translating rough inputs, drawings, and design intent into client-ready experiences that help people understand, approve, and emotionally commit.

Visual strategyMockups3D directionApproval pagesVideo promptsMaterial systemsClient storytellingMarketing assets
Technology Layer

Embedded into the workflow

The technology stays behind the curtain. The client experiences clarity, confidence, and premium presentation.

Fast concept layerImage mockups, material direction, quick environment placement, approval framing.
3D / rendering layerBlender, Rhino/SketchUp/CAD intake, KeyShot/V-Ray/Unreal-style rendering when fidelity is worth it.
Client presentation layerMobile micro-sites, PDF decks, private approval links, annotated visual decision points.
Motion layerSimple inspection videos from approved stills: slow dolly, detail closeups, finish and reflection studies.
Reusable library layerWood, steel, brass, glass, stone, hardware, lighting moods, and recurring architectural components.
Strategic Outcome

The new offer

“We do not just make a 3D render. We build the client visualization experience that helps high-value custom work get understood, approved, and remembered.”
